question: Explain the two phases of Two-Phase Locking with a diagram.
expectedPoints: Growing phase: locks can be acquired but not released
Shrinking phase: locks can be released but not acquired
Lock point: point of maximum locks determines serializability order
After shrinking begins, no new locks can be obtained

question: Differentiate between Shared Lock and Exclusive Lock with examples.
expectedPoints: Shared lock (S-lock): allows reading, multiple transactions can hold simultaneously
Exclusive lock (X-lock): allows reading and writing, only one transaction can hold
Lock compatibility: S compatible with S, X incompatible with S and X
Example: S-lock for SELECT, X-lock for UPDATE operations

question: What is a deadlock? Explain the necessary conditions for deadlock with examples.
expectedPoints: Deadlock: circular wait between transactions for resources
Four conditions: Mutual exclusion, Hold and wait, No preemption, Circular wait
Example: T1 holds A waits for B, T2 holds B waits for A
Can be detected using wait-for graph

question: Explain Strict Two-Phase Locking. How does it prevent cascading rollbacks?
expectedPoints: Strict 2PL: holds exclusive locks until commit/abort
Shared locks can be released in shrinking phase
Prevents cascading rollbacks because other transactions cannot read uncommitted data
All writes become visible only at commit time

question: What is the difference between Strict 2PL and Rigorous 2PL?
expectedPoints: Strict 2PL: holds only X-locks until commit
Rigorous 2PL: holds ALL locks (both S and X) until commit
Rigorous 2PL is stricter than Strict 2PL
Both prevent cascading rollbacks, but Rigorous provides stronger guarantees

question: Explain Conservative Two-Phase Locking and its advantages/disadvantages.
expectedPoints: Conservative 2PL: acquires all needed locks before transaction begins
Advantage: Prevents deadlocks completely
Disadvantage: Requires lock anticipation, reduced concurrency, may cause starvation
Not practical in many real systems due to requiring advance knowledge of locks needed

question: What is the lock compatibility matrix? Draw and explain.
expectedPoints: Matrix showing which locks can be granted when another is held
Rows: requested lock, Columns: held lock
S + S = Yes (compatible)
S + X = No, X + S = No, X + X = No (incompatible)
This determines whether lock requests can be granted

question: What is Two-Phase Locking (2PL)? Explain the protocol with an example schedule. Also prove that 2PL ensures conflict-serializability.
expectedPoints: Definition: Growing phase (lock acquisition) and shrinking phase (lock release)
Example schedule showing lock acquisition and release
Lock point determines serializability order
Proof: If schedule follows 2PL, it is conflict-serializable
Lock point ordering theorem: schedules are serializable in order of lock points
Explain with precedence graph concept

question: Discuss deadlock handling in Two-Phase Locking. Explain deadlock detection using wait-for graph and deadlock prevention techniques.
expectedPoints: Deadlock definition and four necessary conditions
Wait-for graph: nodes = transactions, edge T1→T2 when T1 waits for T2
Cycle in wait-for graph indicates deadlock
Prevention: Wait-die and Wound-wait schemes using timestamps
Detection: Periodic check for cycles in wait-for graph
Recovery: Rollback one or more transactions to break deadlock

question: Compare Timestamp-Based Concurrency Control with Two-Phase Locking. When would you prefer one over the other?
expectedPoints: 2PL: Lock-based, ensures conflict serializability, may cause deadlocks
Timestamp Ordering: Uses timestamps, ensures timestamp-serializable schedules, no deadlocks
2PL: May block transactions causing delays
TO: May reject operations causing restarts
2PL preferred when deadlocks must be avoided through prevention
TO preferred in systems with high conflict rates where restarts are acceptable
TO has no blocking but may cause more transaction restarts

question: Explain the following: (a) Cascading Rollback (b) Starvation (c) Lock Escalation. How does 2PL handle these issues?
expectedPoints: Cascading Rollback: Abort of one transaction requires abort of others who read uncommitted data
Starvation: Transaction waits indefinitely due to repeated priority to other transactions
Lock Escalation: When too many locks held, system escalates to table-level lock
Strict/Rigorous 2PL prevents cascading rollbacks
Starvation handled by timeout or priority schemes
Lock escalation managed by DBMS based on lock thresholds
